title: Global Wind and Convection Cell Model
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Explore Earth's atmospheric circulation with animated convection cells, wind patterns, and Coriolis effect toggle.

educational.topic: Global Atmospheric Circulation and Wind Patterns
learningObjectives: Describe how convection cells, the Coriolis effect, and global wind patterns interact to create Earth's major climate zones
Identify the three convection cells and explain why air rises or sinks at specific latitudes
Explain how the Coriolis effect deflects winds and creates named wind belts
Connect global wind and precipitation patterns to biome distribution
prerequisites: Knowledge that Earth rotates on its axis

keyQuestions: Why are most of the world's deserts located near 30 degrees latitude?
How does the Coriolis effect change the direction of surface winds?
What is the relationship between convection cells and biome distribution?
How might climate change shift the boundaries of convection cells?
commonMisconceptions: Students often think winds blow directly from the poles to the equator without deflection
Many students confuse the Coriolis effect with centrifugal force or believe it directly causes weather
Students may think all latitudes receive equal precipitation
assessmentOpportunities: Have students sketch convection cells from memory after exploring the simulation
Assess student ability to explain the Coriolis effect using the toggle feature
